segments.purgatorio-15-g24: This segment is a single punctuation mark (a period) serving as a pause or line break in the poem; there are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-15-g25: The clause is an admonition introduced by the negative particle 'Non' and directed to the addressee 'ti' (you). 'maravigliar' is an archaic infinitive used like an imperative meaning 'be astonished' (literary usage). 's’ancor' is a contraction for 'se ancor' (if still/yet) and 't’abbaglia' places the clitic object 't’' (you) before the finite verb 'abbaglia' (it/they dazzle), so literally 'if still it dazzles you'. 'la famiglia del cielo' is a poetic phrase meaning 'the company/household of heaven.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g26: 'a me rispuose' is straightforward: the preposition 'a' + indirect object 'me' (to me) followed by the past tense 'rispuose' (responded/answered) — an ordinary perfect/past narrative form.
segments.purgatorio-15-g27: 'messo è' is an impersonal or formal locution using the past participle 'messo' with the copula 'è' to mean 'it has been appointed/ordained' (an idiomatic, somewhat archaic passive sense). The relative clause 'che viene ad invitar ch’om saglia' uses 'viene' (comes) + infinitive 'ad invitar' (to invite/urge) and the fused 'ch’om' (che + om, 'that one' / 'that man' / 'someone'), followed by 'saglia' — an archaic verb-form meaning 'go up/ascend' (infinitival/subjunctive flavor in older usage). Overall: 'it is ordained that one comes to invite a man to ascend.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g28: 'Tosto sarà' is a future ('it will soon be') with 'Tosto' (soon). 'ch’ a veder queste cose' is a subordinating 'che' + infinitive clause 'a veder' (on seeing/to see) + demonstrative 'queste cose' (these things). 'non ti fia grave' uses the future archaic form 'fia' (from 'essere', 'will be') with clitic 'ti' (to you) and predicate adjective 'grave' meaning 'burdensome/serious' — word order places the negation 'non' before the indirect object clitic and verb as typical in older Italian.
segments.purgatorio-15-g29: The contrastive 'ma' (but) introduces the positive result. 'fieti diletto' is a compressed/archaic phrasing meaning 'will be delight to you' (here 'fieti' functions like 'fia ti' or 'si farà a te' — an older fused future construction where the verb and clitic combine), so the object of pleasure is 'ti' (to you) and 'diletto' is the noun 'delight.' 'quanto natura a sentir ti dispuose' is a concessive/comparative clause: 'quanto' = 'as much as' (or 'to the extent that'), 'natura' = nature, 'a sentir' = 'to feel/hear/experience' (infinitive), and 'dispuose' = past tense 'disposed/assigned' — together: 'as much as nature has disposed you to feel.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g30: 'Poi giunti fummo' is a past narrative expression: 'Poi' (then), 'giunti' (having arrived/reached; past participle), and 'fummo' (we were) — together functioning as a compound past 'we came/reached.' 'a l’angel benedetto' marks the goal with the preposition 'a' + definite article contraction 'l’' + noun 'angel' and adjective 'benedetto' (blessed).
segments.purgatorio-15-g31: 'con lieta voce disse' is a simple descriptive clause: preposition 'con' (with) + adjective 'lieta' (cheerful) modifying 'voce' (voice), followed by the simple past 'disse' (he said). The structure describes manner ('with a cheerful voice') before the quoted speech verb.
segments.purgatorio-15-g32: The quoted imperative 'Intrate' (plural 'enter') directs the group. 'quinci' (from here/hither) marks place; 'ad un scaleo vie' = 'onto/at a stair (scaleo) — 'vie' here functions adverbially 'a little way' or 'a little' (poetic 'a way'); 'men che' is a comparative 'less than' with 'li altri' (the others) as the standard and 'eretto' (raised/erected) describing those other stairs. The clause says: 'enter here on a stairway that is erected/raised less than the others.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g33: 'Noi' is the subject pronoun 'we' placed before the verb for emphasis; 'montavam' is the imperfect tense (first-person plural) of montare, meaning 'we were climbing' or 'we climbed' in a continuous/past habitual sense common in narrative.
segments.purgatorio-15-g34: This is a participial phrase modifying the subject: 'già' = 'already'; 'partiti' is a past participle (masculine plural) agreeing with 'noi' and meaning 'having departed/left'; 'di' marks source ('from'); 'linci' is a plural noun (lynxes) and completes the phrase 'having already left the lynxes.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g35: 'e' = 'and', linking what follows; the following is the opening of a quoted chant. 'Beati' is nominative plural 'blessed' used substantively as in liturgical phrases; 'misericordes' is nominative plural 'the merciful' (adjective used as a substantive) and forms the clause of the exclamation.
segments.purgatorio-15-g36: The verb phrase 'fue cantato' is a passive perfect/simple past construction (fu + past participle cantato) meaning 'was sung/was chanted'; the subject is omitted (the chant). 'Retro' is an adverb meaning 'behind' or 'in the rear/afterwards', indicating the chant came from behind.
segments.purgatorio-15-g37: 'e' = 'and' continues the reported chants. 'Godi' is present-tense or vocative exhortation ('rejoice' or 'be glad') addressed to 'tu' (explicit pronoun used for emphasis). 'che' is the relative 'who', and 'vinci' is present-tense 'you conquer/prevail', so the clause reads 'Rejoice, you who conquer!'
segments.purgatorio-15-g38: This segment is only the closing quotation mark and punctuation in the text; it closes the reported speech from the previous lines.
segments.purgatorio-15-g39: 'Lo mio maestro' uses the article 'Lo' before the possessive + noun construction (a common older/poetic pattern) = 'my master'; 'e io' adds 'and I' to form the compound subject. 'soli amendue' ('soli' + 'amendue') qualifies the subject: 'alone, both of us' (amendue = both). 'suso' is an adverb 'up' (motion upward) and 'andavamo' is the imperfect first-person plural 'we were going' (descriptive past action).
segments.purgatorio-15-g40: 'e io pensai' = 'and I thought'; 'pensai' is the passato remoto (or simple past) first-person singular of pensare, narrating a completed mental action: 'I thought' (with 'e io' adding emphasis on the speaker).
segments.purgatorio-15-g41: 'andando' is the gerund of andare and means 'while going' or 'as I went', indicating that the thought occurred during the motion already described (simultaneity).
segments.purgatorio-15-g42: 'prode' is an older adjective meaning 'useful/advantageous/fit' (here impersonal in sense 'it would be useful'); 'acquistar' is the infinitive 'to gain' or 'to obtain'; 'ne' here refers back to 'in' or 'from' his words ('in them' / 'from them'); 'le parole sue' = 'his words' (definite article + noun + possessive). Together: 'that it would be useful to gain [something] from his words.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g43: 'e' = 'and'; 'dirizza’mi' is an archaic/contracted past form with the clitic 'mi' (I directed myself), i.e. 'I turned to him' (the clitic attached after the truncated verb); 'a lui' = 'to him'; 'sì dimandando' is a gerund phrase 'thus asking' or 'asking in this way' (sì = 'thus', dimandando = 'asking').
segments.purgatorio-15-g44: The quoted question begins: 'Che' is the interrogative 'what'; 'volse dir' is an idiomatic past expression meaning 'meant to say' or 'intended to say' (volse is a past form and here governs the infinitive 'dir'); 'lo spirto di Romagna' is the subject 'the spirit (soul) of Romagna.' The clause asks what that spirit meant by his words.
segments.purgatorio-15-g45: 'e' = 'and'; the speaker cites the two terms the spirit used: the quoted 'divieto' and 'consorte'; 'menzionando' is the gerund 'mentioning' and indicates that those two words were mentioned by the spirit. The whole final phrase specifies the words whose meaning the speaker is asking about.
segments.purgatorio-15-g46: This segment is only a punctuation mark (a full stop); there are no words to analyze or translate.
segments.purgatorio-15-g47: Per ch’elli a me: is an introductory conjunctive phrase marking reported speech. Per + ch’ is a poetic contraction equivalent to 'perché' / 'whereupon' or 'so', elli is an archaic subject pronoun ('he'/'they' in older usage), and a me is the indirect-object phrase 'to me' that introduces the following quotation.
segments.purgatorio-15-g48: Di sua maggior magagna conosce il danno is an inversion for emphasis: the genitive phrase 'Di sua maggior magagna' ('of his greater fault') is fronted, and conosce is 3rd-person singular present indicative ('he knows'); il danno is the direct object 'the harm/damage'. 'Magagna' is an archaic noun for a fault or defect.
segments.purgatorio-15-g49: e però non s’ammiri se ne riprende perché men si piagna is a linked clause giving the reason. 'Non s’ammiri' is impersonal/passive-reflexive (si) with ammiri (present-subjunctive/indicative shape used impersonally) meaning 'one should not be surprised'; 'se ne riprende' = 'if he reproves (him) about it' (ne = 'about it/of it'); perché introduces the cause: 'men si piagna' = 'one laments/complains less' (si is reflexive/impersonal).
segments.purgatorio-15-g50: Perché introduces a causal explanation ('because'). 'S’appuntano' is a reflexive/passive-form of appuntare meaning 'are aimed/directed' (3rd-person plural), with i vostri disiri as the subject. 'Dove' is a relative/adverb 'where'; 'per compagnia' means 'through/by companionship'; 'parte si scema' is an intransitive/passive phrasing meaning 'a part is diminished' (si scema = 'is diminished'), i.e. company diminishes part of the desire.
segments.purgatorio-15-g51: invidia move il mantaco a’ sospiri is a simple clause with subject (invidia) and verb (move, 3rd-person singular present 'moves/prompts'). Il mantaco is the direct object (an archaic or dialectal noun; context implies 'the stingy/miser' or an epithet), and a’ sospiri ('to sighs') indicates the effect or direction of the prompting.
segments.purgatorio-15-g52: Ma = 'but' (conjunction). se introduces a conditional clause ('if'). l’amor is the elided article + noun (= 'the love'); de la spera supprema = 'of the supreme sphere' (de is the archaic/poetic form instead of della; spera here means celestial 'sphere', not 'hope'). torcesse is 3rd person singular imperfect subjunctive (torcere) used to express a potential or counterfactual action: 'were to turn' or 'should turn'; in suso = 'upward' (suso is a poetic adverb). il disiderio vostro = 'your desire' with normal article + noun + possessive order.
segments.purgatorio-15-g53: non = negation 'not'. vi is a locative adverb meaning 'there' (used with essere to place something in/at somewhere). sarebbe = conditional 'would be' (3rd sing. of essere). al petto = literal 'at (the) chest/breast', idiomatically 'in your heart/ breast'. quella tema = 'that fear' (quella is the demonstrative modifying tema; tema here = 'fear' in older usage).
segments.purgatorio-15-g54: ché = 'for'/'because', introducing the reason clause that follows.
segments.purgatorio-15-g55: per quanti = 'by/for how many' or 'in proportion to how many'; quanti is an indefinite relative/measure word. si dice is the impersonal/passive si + present of dire: 'are said/called' (literally 'one says' or 'is said'); più lì = 'more there' i.e. 'more (people) there'; 'nostro' in quotes indicates the word 'our' (people there called 'ours'). The whole phrase means roughly 'the more there are who are called “ours”.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g56: tanto sets up a proportional/result clause ('so much' / 'to that extent' — linking back to 'per quanti...'). possiede = present indicative 'possesses' (3rd sing.), with ciascuno as the logical subject 'each one'. più di ben = 'more of good' (più + di + ben = a comparative amount of good/things of goodness); the clause says that, to that extent, each person possesses more goodness.
segments.purgatorio-15-g57: e = 'and'. più di caritate = 'more of charity' (caritate = carità in poetic/Latinized form, i.e. Christian love/charity); arde = present 'burns' (metaphorical: is inflamed with). in quel chiostro = 'in that cloister' (chiostro meaning the cloister or conventual/monastic community; here a heavenly cloister). The full thought: 'and more charity burns in that cloister.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g156: Questo is the demonstrative subject 'this.' Ne is a clitic particle (here partitive/ablative-like) meaning 'from it/from them/from us' and stands before the verb in Dante's word order. Tolse is the passato remoto (simple past) 3rd person singular of togliere, 'took away.' Li occhi is the direct object: li is the archaic masculine plural definite article (modern i) and occhi here means 'sight' or 'eyes' collectively; e coordinates the second object; l' is the elided definite article before a vowel; aere puro is the noun plus postpositive adjective ('pure/clear air' — the adjective follows the noun for emphasis), so both 'li occhi' and 'l'aere puro' are objects removed by the action of the verb.
segments.purgatorio-15-g69: Quello is a demonstrative adjective meaning 'that' and modifies the noun bene. infinito e ineffabil are adjectives placed before bene for poetic emphasis; ineffabil is the archaic/poetic form of ineffabile (dropped final vowel). che introduces a relative clause ('which'); là sù (= lì + sù) is a two-part locative 'there up/above'; è is the 3rd-person singular present of essere, the copula of the relative clause.
segments.purgatorio-15-g70: così is an adverb 'thus/so'. corre is 3rd-person singular present of correre used metaphorically ('flows/moves/runs') and takes the Thing as subject implied from the previous clause. ad + amore = 'toward/to love' (ad used before a vowel). com’ is the elided form of come before a vowel (come a = 'as/like'); lucido modifies corpo ('shining/bright body'). raggio is the subject of the following verb, and vene is the archaic/poetic present 3rd-person singular of venire (equivalent to viene, 'comes'). The order is inverted for metrical/poetic effect: 'as to a shining body a ray comes.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g71: Tanto 'so much' sets the quantity. si dà is a reflexive/middle construction of dare and works impersonally here ('is given' / 'gives itself' / 'it gives'), so the clause reads 'so much is given'. quanto introduces the comparative/relative 'as much as', trova is 3rd-person present of trovare 'finds', and d' (di) ardore means 'of ardor/passion' — i.e. it gives in proportion to the ardor it finds.
segments.purgatorio-15-g72: sì che is a two-word connective meaning 'so that' or 'with the result that', used to introduce the consequence or further explanation of the previous statement.
segments.purgatorio-15-g73: quantunque is a concessive conjunction 'however much'. carità here is the theological/abstract 'charity' or divine love and is the subject. si stende is the pronominal form of stendere ('extends itself' / 'reaches out'), 3rd-person singular present; the reflexive si makes the verb intransitive/reciprocal in effect.
segments.purgatorio-15-g74: cresce is 3rd-person singular present of crescere 'grows/increases'. sovr' is the elided form of sovra/sopra 'over/upon' used before a vowel. essa is a feminine pronoun ('it') referring back to carità. l' etterno valore is the noun phrase 'the eternal value/worth' (etterno is an archaic/poetic spelling); the subject follows the verb for emphasis: 'the eternal value grows over it.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g75: E is 'and'. quanta gente is a quantifier + collective noun 'how many people' (quanta agrees with gente). più là sù 'more up there' (più = 'more', là sù = 'up above'). s'intende is the pronominal/impersonal form of intendere here meaning roughly 'are understood/are present' or 'are to be found' — i.e. 'the more people there are up above'.
segments.purgatorio-15-g76: più v’è is inverted order of 'vi è più' meaning 'there is more'. da introduces the following infinitive phrase in a partitive/relational sense; bene here is the noun 'good' (the Good, i.e. the divine good) and amare is the infinitive 'to love' — together 'there is more of the Good to love' (i.e. more to be loved).
segments.purgatorio-15-g77: e 'and'. più 'more'. vi is the locative/adverbial 'there'. s' is the reflexive/impersonal particle si. ama is 3rd-person singular present of amare 'loves' — together 'and the more they love there' or 'the more one loves there'. The construction is impersonal/collective: 'people love there more.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g78: e 'and'. come 'as' introducing a simile. specchio 'mirror' is the image of the simile. l'uno a l'altro 'one to the other' (the two are reciprocally related; l' is the article elided before a vowel). rende is 3rd-person singular present of rendere 'returns/gives back/renders' — 'and one reflects (itself) to the other like a mirror.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g79: 'E se' = 'And if' (conjunction + conditional marker). 'la mia ragion' = 'my reason' or 'my argument' (subject). 'non' negates the verb. 'ti' is the clitic object/pronoun 'to you'. 'disfama' is a present tense verb (third singular) of an archaic verb 'disfamare' used here in the sense 'put to rest / dispel (a doubt or desire)'; grammatically the verb takes 'ti' as the affected person. The clause means 'And if my argument does not put you to rest / does not dispel your doubt/desire'.
segments.purgatorio-15-g80: 'vedrai' is the future indicative (second person singular) of 'vedere' — 'you will see'. 'Beatrice' is the direct object; placing the verb before the object is normal. This sentence therefore promises that the addressee will see Beatrice.
segments.purgatorio-15-g81: 'ed' = 'and' (e with euphonic d). 'ella' = 'she' (subject pronoun referring to Beatrice). 'pienamente' is an adverb 'completely/fully'. 'ti' is the clitic pronoun 'from you' / 'to you' functioning as indirect object affected by the action. 'torrà' is a future tense (third singular) of an archaic verb 'torre'/'torrere' meaning 'to take away' or 'remove' — here 'she will take away (from you)'. 'questa e ciascun’ altra brama' is the direct object: 'this and each/any other longing' (note the elision in 'ciascun’'). Word order places adverb and clitic before the verb, typical in Dante. The clause means 'and she will entirely remove this and every other desire from you.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g1: Quanto ... tanto is a correlative comparative phrase: Quanto introduces the span being compared (“as much as / the amount that lies between ...”) and links forward to the matching tanto in the next clause. tra + noun phrases marks the interval (“between the end of the third hour and the beginning of the day”); l’ultimar here is an archaic substantival use meaning “the end.” par de la spera is a present-tense clause (“seems from/of the sphere”), and the relative clause che ... scherza modifies spera: che = “which,” sempre a guisa di fanciullo scherza = “always plays/frolics like a child.”
segments.purgatorio-15-g2: This is the matching tanto clause: tanto introduces the result/comparison (“so/therefore”); pareva is an imperfect (past) verb meaning “it seemed.” inver’ la sera = toward evening; essere + past participle rimaso expresses the idea that the sun was left/remained in its course (literally ‘to be remained’). The word order (pareva ... essere ... rimaso) places the perception verb before the infinitive construction expressing what seemed to be the case.
segments.purgatorio-15-g3: A short locative phrase: vespero = vespers / the evening prayer/time, and là = ‘there’ (in that place/region). It states that, on that side, it was evening (i.e. vespers were happening).
segments.purgatorio-15-g4: e = and; qui = here (contrasting with là just before); mezza notte = literally ‘middle night’ (i.e. midnight or the middle of the night); era is the imperfect/past of essere, ‘it was.’ The clause contrasts times at different places: there vespers, and here it was midnight.
segments.purgatorio-15-g5: E = and. i raggi = the rays (subject). ne is a clitic pronoun referring back to the group (here functioning like 'on us' / 'upon us' or 'of them'); ferien is an archaic verbal form (3rd pl. imperfect of ferire) meaning “they struck/struck at.” per mezzo ’l naso = a circumstantial phrase ‘through the middle/bridge of the nose.’
segments.purgatorio-15-g6: perché = because. per noi girato era = a passive/participial construction meaning ‘the mountain had been turned for us’ or ‘the mountain was so turned toward us’; girato is the past participle of girare, and era is the auxiliary ‘was.’ sì ’l monte emphasizes the result (‘so [was] the mountain’). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-15-g7: che introduces a subordinate clause explaining how the mountain was turned: già = already; dritti andavamo = ‘we were going straight’ (andavamo is imperfect first‑person plural of andare); inver’ l’occaso = toward the occident/sunset. The line describes their direction of travel.
segments.purgatorio-15-g8: quand’ io senti’ = ‘when I felt’ (quand’ is contracted quando; senti’ is an archaic past form of sentire). a me gravar la fronte a lo splendore = a causative/infinitival phrase: the splendour was weighing on my brow; gravar is the infinitive ‘to weigh/burden,’ with a me indicating that it affected me. assai più che di prima = a comparative: ‘much more than before.’
segments.purgatorio-15-g9: e connects clauses. Stupor m’eran = a poetic inversion: ‘astonishment was to me’ i.e. ‘I was amazed’; m’ = mi as indirect object. le cose non conte = ‘the things not told/unsaid’ (non conte = not recounted/ unexplained); the plural subject follows the predicate for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-15-g10: ond’ (onde) = ‘whence’ / ‘therefore.’ io levai = first-person past historic ‘I raised.’ le mani = the hands. inver’ la cima de le mie ciglia = ‘toward the top of my eyelids/eyebrows’: inver’ = toward, cima = summit/top, de le mie ciglia = of my eyelids/eyelashes (ciglia here = eyelids/edge near the brow).
segments.purgatorio-15-g11: e = and. fecimi is fece + mi (past ‘made me’ or ‘caused me’): the thing (brightness) caused him a certain effect. ’l solecchio is the direct object: an old/rare noun meaning a kind of ocular discomfort or dazzlement (here ‘a dazzlement’ or ‘eye-soreness’).
segments.purgatorio-15-g12: che = which (relative pronoun referring to the solecchio just mentioned). del soverchio visibile = ‘of the excessive visible’ i.e. caused by excessive visible brightness; soverchio = excessive/exceeding. lima = a rasp/file; figuratively it describes a grinding/irritating effect. The clause defines the nature of the dazzlement as the corroding result of too much visible light.
segments.purgatorio-15-g13: Come quando = ‘as when’ (introducing a simile). da l’acqua o da lo specchio = ‘from the water or from the mirror’ (two possible reflecting surfaces). salta lo raggio = the ray ‘jumps’ or ‘springs’ (salta = present indicative describing a general physical fact) a l’opposita parte = ‘to the opposite side.’ The line sets up an optical simile of reflection/refraction.
segments.purgatorio-15-g14: salendo su = gerund + particle ‘rising up.’ per lo modo parecchio = ‘by a manner quite/considerably similar’ (modo = manner, parecchio = rather/quite). a quel che scende = ‘to that which descends’; the phrase says the upward ray follows roughly the same manner as the descending one.
segments.purgatorio-15-g15: e tanto si diparte = ‘and it departs so much’ (si diparte = separates/deflects). dal cader de la pietra = ‘from the fall of the stone’ (cader = falling motion used as a noun), in igual tratta = ‘in an equal stretch/trajectory’ (tratta here = a stretch/track), describing how the light’s path diverges from a straight fall.
segments.purgatorio-15-g16: sì come = ‘just as’ or ‘as’; mostra = ‘shows’ (present indicative). esperïenza e arte = ‘experience and art/skill’: the poet appeals to both practical observation and learned optics to justify the simile.
segments.purgatorio-15-g17: così mi parve = ‘thus it seemed to me’ (parve = past tense of parere). da luce rifratta = ‘from refracted light’ (rifratta = refracted, participle used adjectivally). quivi dinanzi a me = ‘there before me’; esser percosso = infinitival phrase ‘to be struck’ or ‘to be affected/struck by’ (describing the impression on him).
segments.purgatorio-15-g18: per che = therefore/so that. a fuggir = infinitive ‘to flee’; la mia vista = ‘my sight’ (subject of the infinitival construction). fu ratta = ‘was quick/was snatched away’: fu is past of essere and ratta is an adjective agreeing with vista (feminine), here meaning swift/rapid in the sense that his sight quickly fled/turned away.
segments.purgatorio-15-g19: Che è quel = a direct interrogative: Che = ‘what,’ è = present ‘is,’ quel = ‘that one’ (demonstrative). The phrase begins the question.
segments.purgatorio-15-g20: dolce padre is a vocative address: dolce = ‘dear’/‘sweet’ (term of endearment/respect), padre = ‘father’ (address to the guide, Virgil).
segments.purgatorio-15-g21: a che = ‘to/at which’ (introducing the object of his wonder). non posso = ‘I am not able’ (present tense of potere). schermar lo viso = schermare (infinitive) + direct object lo viso: ‘to shield/screen the face.’ tanto che mi vaglia = ‘so that it avails me’ (tanto che = so much that; mi = to me/for me; vaglia is present subjunctive of valere ‘to be of use’ — here the idea is ‘I cannot screen my face enough for it to do me any good’).
segments.purgatorio-15-g22: diss’ io = contracted past tense ‘I said’ (diss’ = dissi / said), with the subject pronoun io for emphasis. This tags the speech.
segments.purgatorio-15-g23: e = and; pare inver’ noi = pare (present) ‘it seems’ + inver’ = toward, noi = us: ‘and does it seem to be toward us’; esser mosso = infinitive + past participle ‘to be moved’ (i.e. ‘to be moving’ or ‘to be stirred/moving’). The whole is a direct question about apparent motion toward them.
segments.purgatorio-15-g58: Io son d'esser contento più digiuno: 'Io son' = 'I am' (son is the present indicative of essere with io as subject). The sequence 'd'esser' is the contracted preposition di + infinitive essere (a conventional older idiom meaning 'to be' or 'to be inclined to be'); contento is a predicate adjective. 'Più digiuno' has più modifying digiuno (digiuno here = 'fasting'), so the whole phrase means 'I am more content to be fasting.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g59: diss' io: 'diss'' is an apocopated archaic past form of dire (spoken/write form of 'dissi'/'disse') meaning 'I said'; io is the emphatic subject pronoun placed after the verb for stylistic inversion.
segments.purgatorio-15-g60: che se mi fosse pria taciuto: 'che' here introduces the content of what was said ('that'/'that if...'); 'se' is the conditional 'if'; 'mi' is the dative 'to me' (i.e. 'kept from me' or 'kept silent as to me'); 'fosse taciuto' is an archaic perfect-passive sense formed with the subjunctive/imperf. form of essere + past participle (here rendered as a past counterfactual: 'had been kept silent/withheld'); 'pria' = 'earlier'/'before'.
segments.purgatorio-15-g61: e più di dubbio ne la mente aduno: 'e' = 'and'; 'più di dubbio' = 'more of doubt' (i.e. 'more doubt'); 'ne' is a pronominal particle referring back to the matter just mentioned ('about it'/'of it'); 'la mente' = 'the mind'; 'aduno' is first-person present indicative 'I gather/collect' (adunare). Put together: 'and I gather more doubt about it in my mind.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g62: Com' esser puote ch'un ben,: archaic inversion for modern 'Come può essere che un bene...'; 'Com'' (= come) = 'how'; 'esser' = infinitive 'to be'; 'puote' is an archaic third-person singular of potere meaning 'can' (→ 'can be'); 'ch'' = che = 'that'; 'un ben' = 'a good' (bene as a noun, 'a good/benefit'). The line opens an exclamatory/questioning subordinate clause.
segments.purgatorio-15-g63: distributo in più posseditor,: 'distributo' is a past participle modifying 'un ben' ('distributed'); 'in più posseditor' = 'among many possessors' (in + più = among/more, posseditor = possessors/holders). The participle follows the noun in postnominal position, a typical older/poetic word order.
segments.purgatorio-15-g64: faccia più ricchi di sé che se da pochi è posseduto?: 'faccia' is the present subjunctive of fare (third person singular) used in this exclamatory/question clause meaning 'make' or 'could make'; 'più ricchi di sé' = 'richer in itself' or 'make (them) more rich through itself' (di sé = 'of itself'); 'che' here introduces a comparative 'than'; final clause 'se da pochi è posseduto' = 'if it is possessed by few'. Altogether: '...makes (them) richer in itself than if it were possessed by a few.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g65: Single punctuation mark (period) marking the end of the question/line; no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-15-g66: Ed elli a me:: 'Ed' = 'and'; 'elli' here is the poetic/archaic form for 'egli' = 'he'; 'a me' = 'to me' (i.e. 'he said to me' — this is a short stage direction before the quoted speech). The colon marks reported speech.
segments.purgatorio-15-g67: «Però che tu rificchi la mente pur a le cose terrene,: 'Però che' = 'because'/'for that'; 'tu' = second-person singular subject 'you'; 'rificchi' is an archaic present-tense verb form (verb rificcare or similar) meaning roughly 'you fix/fasten/turn' — here 'you fix (or fasten) your mind'; 'la mente' = 'the mind'; 'pur' = 'only/solely'; 'a le cose terrene' = 'on/to earthly things'. So: 'Because you fix your mind only on earthly things.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g68: di vera luce tenebre dispicchi.: 'di vera luce' = 'from/of true light' (di + noun phrase); 'tenebre' = 'darkness' (direct object); 'dispicchi' is an archaic present-tense verb (from dispiccare/dispicchiare) meaning 'you pull off/choose/pick out' or more idiomatically here 'you take/pick darkness from true light' — i.e. you mistake or prefer darkness instead of true light. The verb is second-person singular present, so the whole clause addresses Dante's action.
segments.purgatorio-15-g135: This segment contains only punctuation (a period). There are no words to analyze grammatically; it marks a pause or end of the utterance.
segments.purgatorio-15-g136: "O" is a vocative interjection (an address to someone); "dolce" is an affectionate adjective placed before the noun (poetic placement) and here means "dear/sweet"; "padre" is the noun being addressed (vocative), and "mio" is a possessive placed after the noun for emphasis (poetic, "my").
segments.purgatorio-15-g137: "se" introduces a conditional clause (if); "tu" is the explicit subject pronoun (second person singular, emphatic); "m'" is the clitic object pronoun (short for mi) meaning "to me" placed before the verb; "ascolte" is a present subjunctive or archaic subjunctive form (2nd person singular) meaning "you listen" (used after the conditional marker se in polite/requesting formulas).
segments.purgatorio-15-g138: "io" is the subject pronoun (I) used here for emphasis before the verb; "ti" is the clitic object pronoun (you) placed before the verb; "dirò" is the future indicative (1st person singular) of dire, meaning "I will tell" — it expresses a promised action after the conditional/if-clause.
segments.purgatorio-15-g139: "diss'" is the elided form of the passato remoto 'dissi' (I said) used in narration; the contraction with an apostrophe is a poetic clipping. "io" again is the subject pronoun repeated for emphasis/clarity in direct speech attribution.
segments.purgatorio-15-g140: "ciò che" introduces a nominal relative clause meaning "that which/what"; "m'" (mi) is the indirect object clitic "to me" attached to the verb; "apparve" is the passato remoto (3rd person singular) of apparire, "appeared" (intransitive, here "appeared to me"); "quando" = "when" introduces a temporal clause; "le gambe" is the definite plural subject "the legs"; "mi" (again) is the clitic indicating the experiencer/possessor ("my legs" or "my legs to me" in this construction); "furon" is the passato remoto (3rd person plural) of essere/venire-type auxiliary used here to form the passive-like construction; "sì" = "so/thus"; "tolte" is the past participle of togliere, used adjectivally/passively meaning "taken away/removed." Together: "what appeared to me when my legs were thus taken."
segments.purgatorio-15-g141: "Ed" is the form of "e" used before a vowel (meaning "and"); "ei" is an archaic subject pronoun meaning "he" (used in narrative to mark the speaker). The colon introduces direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-15-g142: "Se" introduces a hypothetical/conditional clause (if); "tu" is the explicit subject pronoun (you); "avessi" is an imperfect subjunctive (2nd person singular) expressing a counterfactual condition "if you had"; "cento" is the numeral "a hundred"; "larve" in medieval/poetic usage means "masks" or "ghostly faces" rather than insect larvae; "sovra" = "over/on" (preposition); "la faccia" = "the face". Word order places the quantity and noun before the locative phrase.
segments.purgatorio-15-g143: "non" negates the clause; "mi" (to me) is an indirect-dative clitic expressing to whom something would be the case; "sarian" is a literary/future-in-the-past/modal periphrasis (they would be) — here a conditional sense "would be"; "chiuse" is a past participle/adjective meaning "closed/hidden"; "le tue cogitazion" is the plural subject "your thoughts/considerations." The structure = "your thoughts would not be closed to me."
segments.purgatorio-15-g144: "quantunque" is conjunctive meaning "although/however much/however it may be" and here qualifies the previous assertion (a concessive afterthought); "parve" is the passato remoto (3rd person singular) of parere, here impersonal/short form meaning "it seemed" or "however it may seem."
segments.purgatorio-15-g145: The clause opens with "Ciò che vedesti" (a nominal clause: "what you saw" — vedesti is passato remoto, 2nd person singular "you saw"). "fu" is passato remoto of essere, giving "was/happened." "perché" = "because." "non scuse d'aprir lo core" here means essentially "[you] had no excuse to refrain from opening the heart" (scuse = pretexts/excuses; d' = di, linking to the infinitive), with "aprir" an infinitive "to open" and "lo core" the direct object "the heart." "a l'acque de la pace" = "to the waters of peace" (a + definite plural noun); the relative clause "che da l'etterno fonte son diffuse" modifies "acque": "which from the eternal source are spread/poured forth" — "son" = sono (present), and "diffuse" is a past participle/adjectival form meaning "spread/poured out." Overall: the seeing occurred because there was no excuse not to open oneself to these peaceful waters that flow from the eternal fountain.
segments.purgatorio-15-g82: Procaccia pur is an exhortation: procaccia is an older/dialectal verb form meaning “procure/see to it” (not the noun sense listed), and pur is an emphatic particle (“indeed/just/even”). che introduces a subordinate clause with the present subjunctive sieno (archaic form of modern siano) because of the volitive/command force; tosto is an adverb “soon,” and spente is the past participle used adjectivally (“extinguished/healed”).
segments.purgatorio-15-g83: come introduces a comparison “as/like”; son is the contracted poetic form of sono (“are”); già is the adverb “already”; le is the feminine plural article 'the' referring forward to 'due' (the two [already healed]).
segments.purgatorio-15-g84: le cinco piaghe is the noun phrase 'the five wounds' (le = article, cinque omitted from the word-list but implied, piaghe = plural of piaga). Piaghe here means literal wounds/bleeding afflictions (stigmata-like wounds) rather than a metaphorical scourge.
segments.purgatorio-15-g85: che is a relative pronoun 'which/that' referring back to 'le cinque piaghe'; si is the reflexive/pronominal marker used with richiudon (they close up themselves); richiudon is an older plural present form (richiudono) meaning 'close again/close up'; per + esser is causal/teleological here 'by/for being' used to mean 'because they are' and dolente is an adjective 'painful/sore', giving the sense 'which close up again because they are sore/painful.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g86: Com' (come) introduces a circumstantial clause 'as/just when'; io voleva dicer uses the imperfect voleva ('I was wishing/was about to') plus the infinitive dicer (old spelling of dire) to mean 'I was about to say.' The quoted Tu m'appaghe is direct speech: Tu = you, m' = me (clitic), and appaghe is an archaic/poetic form of 'appaghi/appaga' meaning 'you satisfy me.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g87: vidimi is a fused form of vidi + mi ('I saw myself' or 'I found myself'), with giunto as a past participle used adjectivally ('arrived'); in su is the prepositional phrase 'up into/on to'; l' altro girone = 'the other/next ring' (girone = circle/ring of the mountain). The whole construction yields 'I found myself brought up into the next ring.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g88: sì che introduces a result clause 'so that'; tacer is the infinitive 'to be silent' used here as the verb in the result clause; mi is the clitic object 'me'; fer is an archaic/poetic causative 'made' (compare modern fare/fece) meaning 'caused/made me'; the grammatical subject le luci vaghe follows (the vague/uncertain lights) and is what caused the silencing — 'so that the vague lights made me be silent.' Note the somewhat inverted word order: infinitive + clitic + causative verb + subject.
segments.purgatorio-15-g89: Ivi 'there' + mi parve 'it seemed to me' (parve = past of parere); in una visïone estatica 'in an ecstatic vision' qualifies the perception; di sùbito 'suddenly'; esser tratto is the infinitive passive construction 'to have been drawn' (tratto = past participle of trarre, 'pulled/drawn'), so the verb parve takes these infinitives as its complements: 'it seemed to me, in an ecstatic vision, to have suddenly been drawn.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g90: e vedere continues the complements to parve: 'and to see' (infinitive); in un tempio 'in a temple'; più persone = 'more people' or 'several people' (più here means 'several/many' in this context). The infinitives Essere tratto and vedere together describe what seemed to happen to the narrator.
segments.purgatorio-15-g91: e una donna is a simple coordinate noun phrase 'and a woman' (introducing one of the people seen).
segments.purgatorio-15-g92: in su l'entrar is a temporal/locative phrase 'upon entering': in su (on/upon) plus l'entrar, a nominalized infinitive 'the entering' (entrar = older infinitive form of entrare). It indicates the woman's action as she comes in.
segments.purgatorio-15-g93: con atto dolce di madre dicer: = 'with a sweet gesture of a mother, (she) said:' con + noun phrase atto dolce di madre = 'with a gentle/motherly gesture'; dicer is the infinitive 'to say' used here as a literary way to introduce the following direct speech (colon follows).
segments.purgatorio-15-g94: «Figliuol mio, is the vocative address 'My son,' with the archaic form figliuol (figliol/figlio) and mio 'my' following in typical poetic word order.
segments.purgatorio-15-g95: perché introduces a direct question 'why'; hai tu così verso noi fatto is an example of emphatic/inverted word order: hai ... fatto is the present perfect 'have you done/made,' with the subject pronoun tu inserted after the auxiliary for emphasis; così 'thus/so', verso noi 'toward us', and fatto is the past participle 'done/acted' — overall 'Why have you acted toward us in this way?'
segments.purgatorio-15-g96: Ecco is an interjection 'behold/here is' used to call attention to what follows.
segments.purgatorio-15-g97: dolenti is a vocative/adjectival plural 'sorrowful/mournful ones' addressing the people present; it agrees in number with the addressees.
segments.purgatorio-15-g98: lo tuo padre e io ti cercavamo: lo is an archaic article variant of il before a possessive (lo tuo = your), tuo padre 'your father'; e io 'and I'; ti is the accusative clitic 'you'; cercavamo is the imperfect 'we were seeking/looking for' — together, 'your father and I were looking for you.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g146: Non + verb forms the negation: 'Non dimandai' is the 1st person singular perfect (or historic) of 'dimandare' (to ask), meaning 'I did not ask'. The quoted 'Che hai?' is direct speech; 'Che' is an interrogative 'what', and 'hai' is the 2nd person singular present of 'avere' used idiomatically ('how do you feel/what's the matter').
segments.purgatorio-15-g147: 'per' introduces a circumstantial/explanatory phrase ('for' or 'in respect of'); 'quel che' is a relative phrase 'that which' and 'face' is the 3rd person singular (present) of 'facere' used here as 'does' (poetic spelling). The clause 'quel che face chi guarda pur con l’occhio che non vede' describes a general situation: 'what happens to someone who looks only with the eye that does not see'.
segments.purgatorio-15-g148: 'quando' = 'when'; 'disanimato' is a past participle used adjectivally meaning 'deprived of spirit' or 'disanimated' and modifies 'il corpo'; 'giace' is present (or historic) 'lies' (from 'giacere'). The order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: 'when the body, disanimated, lies'.
segments.purgatorio-15-g149: 'ma' = 'but'; 'dimandai' is again 1st singular past of 'dimandare' ('I asked'). 'per' + infinitive phrase expresses purpose ('in order to'); 'darti' = infinitive 'dare' + enclitic 'ti' ('to give you') and is the object of 'per'; 'forza al piede' literally 'strength to the foot' = 'strength for your step'. Note 'al' = a + il.
segments.purgatorio-15-g150: 'così' = 'thus/so'; 'frugar' is an infinitive (poetic form) 'to rummage/search about'; 'conviensi' is an impersonal or 3rd‑person singular form from 'conviene' with archaic ending -si ('it is fitting' / 'one must'); the logical subject follows: 'i pigri' ('the lazy'). The clause means 'thus the lazy ought to rummage/search'.
segments.purgatorio-15-g151: 'lenti' (adjective) qualifies 'i pigri' and means 'slow' or 'reluctant'; 'ad' + infinitive 'usar' = 'to use/apply' (here 'to put into practice' their vigil); 'lor' is the archaic enclitic possessive 'loro' ('their'); 'vigilia' = 'wakefulness/vigil'; 'quando riede' = 'when it returns' ('riede' is 3rd singular present of 'riedere'/'ritornare' used impersonally of the vigil returning). The whole phrase: 'slow to put their wakefulness to use when it returns.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g152: 'Noi andavam' is the 1st person plural imperfect 'we were going' (continuous past). 'per lo vespero' is a temporal phrase meaning 'at/around evening' (per + article + noun used poetically to mark time).
segments.purgatorio-15-g153: 'attenti' (adjective) modifies 'Noi' = 'attentive/intent'; 'oltre quanto' = 'beyond how far/as much as'; 'potean' is archaic imperfect 3rd plural of 'potere' = 'they could'; the reflexive infinitive 'allungarsi' ('to stretch oneself') describes the eyes' ability; 'contra' here functions 'toward/against' the 'raggi serotini e lucenti' ('evening and shining rays'). The clause means we strained our eyes farther than they could reach toward the evening light.
segments.purgatorio-15-g154: 'Ed ecco' = 'And behold' or 'and then behold' (exclamatory/narrative marker). 'a poco a poco' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'little by little' or 'gradually' (repeated 'a poco' emphasizes gradualness). 'un fummo' = 'a smoke' (note: 'fummo' here is the noun 'fumo' in Dantean spelling). 'farsi' with the noun expresses reflexive/inchoative formation: 'to make itself / to form' — so 'un fummo farsi' = 'a smoke began to form'. 'verso di noi' = 'toward us' (direction). 'come la notte oscuro' is a simile 'dark as the night', with 'oscuro' agreeing in gender/number with 'fummo' (masculine singular). The verb implied by the construction is intransitive/inchoative: 'a smoke began to form toward us, dark as night.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g155: 'né' = 'nor' (negative conjunction). The phrase 'da quello era loco' literally 'from that was place' follows an idiom 'non/né era luogo da + infinitive' meaning 'there was no place to ...' or 'it afforded no place for ...'. Here 'da quello' = 'from that' / 'in that' (referring to the smoke). 'era' is the imperfect of 'essere' ('there was'). 'loco' = 'place'. The second 'da' before 'cansarsi' introduces the infinitive construction 'da cansarsi' = 'to get tired / to be wearied' (reflexive infinitive). Altogether: 'nor was there in it any place to grow tired' — i.e. the smoke offered no place/condition in which one could become/feel weary. Grammatically, note the idiomatic 'loco da + infinitive' and the doubled 'da' with different functions ('from/in' + infinitive marker).
segments.purgatorio-15-g118: This segment contains only a punctuation mark (a period); there are no lexical items to analyse.
segments.purgatorio-15-g119: E (and) begins the clause; lui is the third-person pronoun 'him/he' and is the logical object of vedea in the perspective of the narrator; vedea is the imperfect of vedere used with an infinitive construction (vedea + chinarsi) meaning 'I saw him do something'; chinarsi is the reflexive infinitive 'to bend/stoop' (si attached in the infinitive) and is the action witnessed by the narrator.
segments.purgatorio-15-g120: per introduces the cause or motive ('because of' / 'through'); la morte is the definite noun 'the death' as the cause; che is a relative pronoun linking to 'morte'; l' is the clitic object pronoun ('him/it') attached to aggravava; aggravava is the imperfect of aggravare meaning 'was making heavier/was afflicting' (ongoing past action), and già 'already' emphasizes that the effect was already in force.
segments.purgatorio-15-g121: inver' is a contracted/poetic form meaning 'toward' or 'against' (here 'toward'); la terra is 'the earth' — together this modifies chinarsi: 'stoop toward the earth.' The apostrophe marks the truncated form typical of Dante's versification.
segments.purgatorio-15-g122: ma 'but' contrasts the previous idea; de li occhi ('from the eyes') uses the older preposition de + article li to indicate source; facea is the imperfect of fare 'made/caused' and here describes a continuous or habitual action ('he made/turned ...'); sempre 'always'; al ciel 'to heaven' (ciel = heaven); porte is used metaphorically as 'doors/gates' (literal lemma shown is porto, but here Dante means 'doors/gates' — i.e. openings of the eyes lifted toward heaven). The whole phrase means that, although death bent him earthward, he continually turned his eyes as gates toward heaven.
segments.purgatorio-15-g123: orando is the present participle of orare 'praying', indicating an action concurrent with the stooping; a introduces the indirect object; l'alto Sire = 'the High Lord' (alto = 'high/lofty', Sire here refers to God), with the apostrophe marking elision.
segments.purgatorio-15-g124: in tanta guerra is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in so great a struggle/contest'; guerra in this moral/religious context means 'struggle' rather than literal war, and tanta intensifies it.
segments.purgatorio-15-g125: che introduces the content of the prayer ('that'); perdonasse is the imperfect subjunctive of perdonare used to state the petition or wished-for action ('that he would forgive'); a marks the indirect object 'to'; suoi = 'his' (referring back to the person praying); persecutori = 'persecutors'. The imperfect subjunctive here conveys the hoped-for forgiveness in indirect discourse.
segments.purgatorio-15-g126: con 'with' links to the manner of the prayer; quello aspetto = 'that expression/look'; che is a relative pronoun introducing the clause; pietà here means 'pity/compassion' (an emotional quality); diserra is a present-tense verb from disserrare 'to unbar/unbolt' used metaphorically: 'the look which pity unbars' (i.e. a look opened by pity). The clause describes the expression accompanying his prayer.
segments.purgatorio-15-g127: Quando is the temporal conjunction 'when'; l'anima mia 'my soul' is the subject; tornò is the passato remoto of tornare 'returned'; di fori is an archaic locution meaning 'from outside' (di + fori); a introduces the destination ('to'); le cose 'the things'; che son 'that are' (son = sono, present tense); fuor di lei 'outside of it' (lei refers back to l'anima, feminine); vere 'true' (feminine plural to agree with cose). The clause describes a change of attention: the soul returning from outward distraction to things truly external to it.
segments.purgatorio-15-g128: io 'I' (subject) + riconobbi is the passato remoto of riconoscere 'I recognized/acknowledged'; i miei 'my' (plural) modifies errori; non falsi literally 'not false' — an emphatic way of saying 'genuine' or 'real' errors; errori is the plural noun 'errors/mistakes.' The phrase 'i miei non falsi errori' therefore means 'my errors that were not false' i.e. true/real mistakes.
segments.purgatorio-15-g129: Lo is the masculine definite article (the); duca here means 'duke' but functionally 'guide' (Virgil) in the poem; mio 'my' is the possessive. Together 'Lo duca mio' is the topic/subject 'my guide.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g130: che introduces a relative clause 'who'; mi 'me' is the object of vedere; potea is the imperfect of potere 'could' (ability in the past); vedere + infinitive (vedere far) means 'to see someone do something' — here 'see me do/act'; far sì com' om = 'make/act thus like a man'; com' is a contraction of come 'as/like'; om = uomo 'man'; che introduces another relative clause; dal sonno 'from sleep'; si slega is reflexive present ('is/gets unbound/loosened') — so the whole relative clause says the guide could see him behaving like a man being released from sleep.
segments.purgatorio-15-g131: disse is the passato remoto of dire 'said' and introduces the direct speech that follows.
segments.purgatorio-15-g132: «Che hai» is an idiomatic interrogative meaning 'what's the matter with you' (literally 'what have you'); the second che begins a subordinate clause; non ti puoi tenere literally 'you cannot hold yourself' — here meaning 'you cannot steady/compose yourself' or 'you cannot keep yourself in control'; puoi is present indicative of potere 'can', tenere 'to hold/keep.'
segments.purgatorio-15-g133: ma 'but' introduces the next question; se' is the archaic/poetic contraction of sei (second-person singular) used here with venuto (past participle) to mean 'you have come' (i.e. 'are come'); più che mezza lega is a quantitative phrase 'more than half a league' (che = 'than' in the comparative); velando li occhi and con le gambe avvolte are participial phrases ('with your eyes veiled' and 'with your legs wrapped') describing his posture while coming. Note Dante's older morphology (se' for sei).
segments.purgatorio-15-g134: a guisa di cui = 'in the manner of one whom' (a guisa = 'in the manner/way'); vino o sonno piega literally 'wine or sleep bends' — piega is present tense of piegare 'to bend' used impersonally of wine or sleep overcoming a person. The clause compares the pilgrim's appearance to that of someone bent or overcome by wine or sleep.
segments.purgatorio-15-g99: 'E' = 'and'. 'come' here is temporal 'when/as'. 'qui' means 'at this point' (an adverb of place/time). 'si tacque' is the reflexive/intransitive use of tacere in the passato remoto (tacque) with 'si' marking the reflexive/impersonal action: 'he/it fell silent'. The phrase links as a temporal clause to the following line.
segments.purgatorio-15-g100: 'ciò che' = 'that which' (a neuter demonstrative + relative pronoun). 'pareva' is the imperfect of parere ('seemed, appeared'), marking an ongoing or prior appearance. 'prima' is an adverb 'formerly/previously' and locates the previous appearance in time.
segments.purgatorio-15-g101: Single-word line 'dispario' is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'disappeared/vanished' (equivalent to modern 'disparve'). Grammatically it is a verbal/predicative form completing the clause 'ciò che pareva prima'.
segments.purgatorio-15-g102: 'Indi' = 'then'. 'm’ apparve' = 'appeared to me' (apparve = passato remoto of apparire; m' = mi, indirect object). 'un’altra' = 'another (woman)'. 'con quell’ acque giù per le gote' = 'with those waters down her cheeks' (quell' acque = those tears; 'giù per le gote' = 'down the cheeks'). 'che ’l dolor distilla' is a relative clause: 'which sorrow distils' (distilla = present tense, general/poetic statement). 'quando di gran dispetto in altrui nacque' gives the time/origin: 'when it arose from great spite in another' (nacque = passato remoto 'arose').
segments.purgatorio-15-g103: 'e' = 'and'. 'dir' is the infinitive 'to say' used here as the verb introducing direct speech ('and [she] said:'). This is a common poetic shorthand: 'e dir:' = 'and she said:'.
segments.purgatorio-15-g104: Direct speech begins. 'Se tu se’' is the conditional 'if you are' (note the archaic duplication of 'se'). 'sire' = 'lord'. 'de la villa' = 'of the village'. 'del cui nome' = 'of whose name'. 'ne’ dèi' is an archaic locution (contextually 'in those days' or 'among those [times/people]'); here it introduces where/when the following dispute took place. 'fu' = passato remoto of essere 'there was'. 'tanta lite' = 'such a quarrel'.
segments.purgatorio-15-g105: 'e' = 'and'. 'onde' here is an adverb/relative meaning 'whence' or 'from which' (not the literal 'wave'); it gives source/reason. 'ogne scïenza' = 'every knowledge/learning'. 'disfavilla' is a present indicative (poetic) meaning 'sends forth sparks' or 'kindles into flame' — a vivid image of intellectual excitement or controversy. The whole clause further characterizes the place named above.
segments.purgatorio-15-g106: 'vendica te' is an imperative: 'avenge yourself' or 'vindicate yourself' (vendica is a command addressed to 'tu'). 'di quelle braccia ardite' = 'against those bold/daring arms' (di = 'against'/'on'). 'ch’abbracciar nostra figlia' is a relative clause; 'ch’ abbracciar' uses an infinitive ('abbracciare') in a poetic/archaic construction to indicate the action 'which embraced our daughter.' The clause names the offence to be avenged.
segments.purgatorio-15-g107: 'o' here is a vocative exclamation ('O Pisistràto') addressing Pisistratus by name; the name is given in direct address. The line closes the quoted speech.
segments.purgatorio-15-g108: 'E' = 'and'. '’l segnor' = 'the lord' (the article + segnor). 'mi parea' = 'seemed to me' (parea = imperfect of parere; mi = to me). The clause sets Dante's impression of the speaker who follows.
segments.purgatorio-15-g109: Two adjectives describing 'il signor': 'benigno' = 'kind/favorable' and 'mite' = 'meek/gentle'. They function as a descriptive apposition. Note: 'mite' here is the adjective 'gentle' (the listed senses did not include this modern meaning).
segments.purgatorio-15-g110: 'risponder lei' is inverted word order: 'lei' (she) is the subject and 'risponder' is the verb; in this poetic register the verb appears in a truncated or archaic form but the sense is 'she answered'. 'con viso temperato' = 'with a tempered/moderate expression' (con + noun + adjective).
segments.purgatorio-15-g111: Direct speech. 'Che' = 'what' (introducing a question: 'what shall'). 'farem' = future of fare ('we shall do'). 'noi' = 'we'. 'a chi' = 'to/to whom' or 'against one who'. 'mal' = 'evil/harm' here used as a noun 'ill' (wishes ill). 'ne' refers back to 'us' (wishes ill of us). 'disira' = present/future sense 'wishes for/longs for' (here 'wishes us harm'). The clause asks rhetorically what they should do to someone who wishes them harm.
segments.purgatorio-15-g112: 'se' = 'if' introducing the conditional subordinate clause. 'quei che' = 'the one who' (quei = he who/the one who). 'ci ama' = 'loves us' (ci = us; ama = loves). 'è per noi condannato' = 'is condemned on our account' or 'is condemned because of us' ('è' = is; 'per' = here 'because of/on account of'; 'noi' = us; 'condannato' = condemned). The question balances mercy/justice: what to do when the beloved is condemned.
segments.purgatorio-15-g113: This segment is a single punctuation mark (a comma) that links the previous speech to the continuing narrative; no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-15-g114: 'Poi' = 'then/afterwards'. 'vidi' = 'I saw' (passato remoto of vedere). 'genti accese in foco d’ira' = 'people inflamed/aflame in the fire of anger' (accese is a past participle/adjective 'inflamed'). 'con pietre un giovinetto ancider' = 'with stones (they) killed a young man' — 'ancider' is an archaic infinitive 'to kill' used verbally in the narrative (poetic infinitive standing in for a finite past form), and the word order places the instrument ('con pietre') before the object for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-15-g115: 'forte' here is an adverb meaning 'loudly' or 'with force'. 'gridando' is the present participle 'shouting/crying out'. 'a sé' = 'to himself'. 'pur' = 'still/yet' (an emphatic particle). Together they describe the young man's loud, repeated cry even as he is being stoned.
segments.purgatorio-15-g116: First vocative/exclamation: 'Martira,' — an address/exclamation naming the victim as a 'martyr' or lamenting 'O martyr'. In context it is his cry or the cry directed at him.
segments.purgatorio-15-g117: Repetition 'martira!' reinforces the cry; here the lemma for the token is the verb-form 'martirare' but the sense conveyed is the vocative/exclamatory 'martyr!'. This is direct speech closing the scene's cry.

sentences.purgatorio-15-s6: "Do not be amazed if the heavenly company still dazzles you," he answered me; "a messenger has been sent who comes to bid a man go up."
sentences.purgatorio-15-s7: Soon, when you see these things, they will not be burdensome to you, but will give you delight according to how nature has disposed you to feel.
sentences.purgatorio-15-s8: Then we came to the blessed angel; with a cheerful voice he said: "Enter here onto a stairway that rises less high than the others erected."
sentences.purgatorio-15-s9: We were climbing, already having left the lynxes, and "Beati misericordes!"
sentences.purgatorio-15-s10: "...was chanted behind, and 'Rejoice, you who conquer!'"
sentences.purgatorio-15-s12: My master and I alone, the two of us, were going up; and I thought, as we went, that it would be useful to gain something from his words; and I turned to him, asking thus: "What did the spirit of Romagna mean, mentioning 'divieto' and 'consorte'?"
sentences.purgatorio-15-s14: Whereupon he to me: “He knows the harm of his greater fault; and therefore one should not be surprised if he reproves him about it, because he mourns less.”
sentences.purgatorio-15-s15: Because your desires are directed where, by companionship, part of them is diminished, envy prompts the miser to sighs.
sentences.purgatorio-15-s16: But if the love of the supreme sphere were to turn your desire upward, that fear would not be in your breast; for, in proportion to how many are called 'our' there, each one possesses more good, and in that cloister burns more charity.
sentences.purgatorio-15-s45: This took from us our sight and the pure air.
sentences.purgatorio-15-s21: That infinite and ineffable Good which is up above,
sentences.purgatorio-15-s22: It gives as much as it finds of ardor;
sentences.purgatorio-15-s23: And the more people there are up above, the more there is to love and the more they love there; and each mirrors the other like a mirror.
sentences.purgatorio-15-s24: And if my reasoning does not dispel you, you will see Beatrice, and she will completely take this and every other longing from you.
sentences.purgatorio-15-s1: As much as lies between the end of the third hour and the beginning of the day — as the sphere that always frolics like a child appears — so it already seemed toward evening that the sun had remained in its course; there vespers, and here it was the middle of the night.
sentences.purgatorio-15-s2: And the rays struck us across the bridge of the nose, because the mountain had been turned for us in such a way that we were already going straight toward the west, when I felt the splendour press my brow much more than before, and things untold were a wonder to me; so I raised my hands toward the top of my eyelids, and that made for me a kind of dazzlement, which is the rasping result of excessive visible brightness.
sentences.purgatorio-15-s3: As when from the water or from the mirror the ray springs to the opposite side, rising up in much the same way as that which descends, and so it departs from the fall of the stone along an equal path, as experience and art show; thus it seemed to me, from refracted light, to be struck there before me; and so my sight was quick to flee.
sentences.purgatorio-15-s4: "What is that, sweet father, which I cannot so shield my face that it avails me," I said, "and does it seem to be moving toward us?"
sentences.purgatorio-15-s17: “I am more content to be fasting,” I said, “than if it had been kept from me earlier, and I gather more doubt about it in my mind.”
sentences.purgatorio-15-s18: How can it be that a good, distributed among many possessors, makes them richer in itself than if it is possessed by a few?
sentences.purgatorio-15-s20: And he to me: “Because you fix your mind solely on earthly things, you take darkness from true light.”
sentences.purgatorio-15-s38: “O my sweet father, if you will listen to me, I will tell you,” I said, “what appeared to me when my legs were thus taken.”
sentences.purgatorio-15-s39: And he: “If you had a hundred masks over your face, your thoughts would not be closed to me, however it may seem.”
sentences.purgatorio-15-s40: What you saw happened because you had no excuse not to open your heart to the waters of peace that are poured forth from the eternal source.
sentences.purgatorio-15-s25: See to it, then, that the five wounds be quickly healed, as the two already are — the five wounds which close up again because they ache.
sentences.purgatorio-15-s26: As I was about to say 'You satisfy me,' I found myself brought up into the next ring, so that the uncertain lights made me fall silent.
sentences.purgatorio-15-s27: There, in an ecstatic vision, it seemed to me that I had suddenly been drawn, and to see several people in a temple; and a woman, upon entering, with the sweet gesture of a mother said, 'My son, why have you dealt with us thus?'
sentences.purgatorio-15-s28: Behold, mournful ones, your father and I were seeking you.
sentences.purgatorio-15-s41: I did not ask, “What ails you?”
sentences.purgatorio-15-s42: — not for what one does who looks only with the eye that does not see, when the body, robbed of spirit, lies — but I asked so as to give you strength to your foot: thus the lazy must rummage, slow to put their vigil to use when it returns.
sentences.purgatorio-15-s43: We were going at evening, intent beyond how far the eyes could stretch toward the evening and shining rays.
sentences.purgatorio-15-s44: And behold, little by little a smoke began to form toward us, dark as the night; nor from that was there any place to grow tired.
sentences.purgatorio-15-s34: And I saw him stoop, because death, which already oppressed him, was drawing him toward the earth; yet from his eyes he ever made gates to heaven, praying to the High Lord, in so great a struggle, that he would forgive his persecutors, with that look which pity unlocks.
sentences.purgatorio-15-s35: When my soul returned from outward things to the things that are truly outside of it, I recognized my errors, not false ones.
sentences.purgatorio-15-s36: My guide, who could see me behaving like a man who is loosened from sleep, said: 'What ails you that you cannot steady yourself? Have you come more than half a league with your eyes veiled and your legs wrapped, in the manner of one whom wine or sleep bends?'
sentences.purgatorio-15-s29: And when he fell silent there, what had seemed before vanished.
sentences.purgatorio-15-s30: Then another appeared to me, with those waters running down her cheeks that sorrow distils when it springs from great spite in another, and said: 'If you are lord of the village whose name in those days was the subject of such strife, and from which every knowledge sent forth sparks, avenge yourself on those bold arms that embraced our daughter, O Pisistratus.'
sentences.purgatorio-15-s31: And the lord seemed to me, kind and gentle, to answer her with a tempered expression: 'What shall we do to one who wishes us ill, if the one who loves us is condemned on our account?''
sentences.purgatorio-15-s32: Then I saw people, inflamed by the fire of anger, stone a young man to death, loudly crying out to himself still: 'Martyr, martyr!'
